definition: 'Bubble vs. revolution' is a false binary. The historical modal outcome for general-purpose technologies is 'value-creating AND investor-destroying.' Railroad era: $4.6B investment (~$150B 2025$), 192 railroads failed in 1893, AND transportation costs fell 90%+. Telecom: ~$2T investor capital destroyed AND $20T+ digital economy infrastructure created. AI's current capex-to-investor-return ratio (4-6x) is within the historical range of railroads (3-5x) and telecom (5-8x).
evidence: Railroad era: $4.6B invested, 192 railroads failed 1893, transportation costs fell 90%+
Telecom: ~$2T destroyed AND $20T+ digital economy infrastructure created
Capex-to-investor-return ratios: railroads 3-5x, telecom 5-8x, AI current 4-6x
pair_3: 'the single most important framing correction'
open_questions: Does the value-creating, investor-destroying scenario have distinct FX implications from the standard bimodal framework?
Can the self-funded nature of hyperscaler capex produce a different outcome than the debt-funded historical analogues?
What is the timeline from value creation to investor destruction in the AI case?
